name: LeNet-5
problem: Fully connected neural networks (MLPs) were used for image recognition, which ignored the spatial layout of pixels and suffered from parameter explosion as image resolution scaled.
innovation: Introduced shared weights (convolutions) and spatial downsampling (subsampling/pooling) directly into a hierarchical network.
keyIdea: Weight sharing forces the network to learn translation-invariant local features, while pooling reduces spatial sensitivity.
advantages: Significant reduction in parameter footprint compared to fully connected networks
Robustness to small translations and distortions of input characters
limitations: Limited to small, simple grayscale images (e.g., 32x32 handwritten digits)
Prone to vanishing gradients when trying to stack deeper layers
legacy: Established the fundamental CNN pattern (stacked Conv-Pool layers followed by Fully Connected heads) used for the next three decades.

name: AlexNet
problem: CNNs were widely considered too slow and memory-intensive to scale to large, high-resolution datasets like ImageNet, leading the vision community to favor hand-crafted features (SIFT/HOG).
innovation: Leveraged parallel GPU training, ReLU non-linear activations for fast convergence, and dropout regularization to prevent overfitting.
keyIdea: Scale up CNN depth and width using hardware acceleration (GPUs) and non-saturating activation functions.
advantages: First model to dramatically outperform classical computer vision on large-scale datasets
RelU activation resolved training saturation issues seen with sigmoid/tanh
limitations: Large parameter footprint (60M+) concentrated heavily in the final fully connected layers
Very coarse early layers (11x11, stride 4 convolutions) that missed fine details
legacy: Triggered the modern deep learning revolution, proving the viability of large neural networks and establishing GPUs as standard hardware.

name: VGG Family
problem: AlexNet's structure was heterogeneous and arbitrary (alternating 11x11, 5x5, and 3x3 filters), making it difficult to systematically design deeper networks without optimization issues.
innovation: Standardized the entire architecture using homogeneous stacks of small 3x3 convolutions and 2x2 max pooling.
keyIdea: Stacking multiple small 3x3 filters achieves the same receptive field as a larger filter (e.g., two 3x3s equal a 5x5) but with fewer parameters and more non-linearities.
advantages: Extremely clean, modular design that was easy to scale up to 19 layers
Provided highly generalizable pre-trained features that served as backbones for many tasks
limitations: Massive computational cost and parameter size (138M+ parameters for VGG16)
High memory consumption in the first few convolutional layers during forward passes
legacy: Proved that network depth is a primary dimension of model capacity and representation learning.

name: ResNet Family
problem: As networks grew deeper (beyond 20 layers), they suffered from 'degradation' where training accuracy saturated and then dropped, because gradients vanished or exploded during backpropagation.
innovation: Introduced residual learning via identity skip connections that bypass one or more convolutional layers.
keyIdea: Let stacked layers fit a residual mapping F(x) = H(x) - x, allowing the network to easily learn identity mappings (H(x) = x) if no refinement is needed.
advantages: Solved the optimization degradation problem, allowing models to scale to 1000+ layers
Identity shortcuts allow gradients to flow directly back to early layers during backpropagation
limitations: Identity skip connections do not add capacity themselves, requiring more conv layers to increase representation power
Can suffer from feature redundancy where layers learn identical representations
legacy: Skip connections became the standard structural component of nearly all subsequent deep learning architectures (Transformers, CNNs).

name: DenseNet Family
problem: ResNet's element-wise addition of identity shortcuts could wash out or dilute features across layers, requiring wider networks or deeper blocks to compensate.
innovation: Introduced dense blocks where each layer receives the concatenated feature maps of all preceding layers as its input.
keyIdea: Maximize feature reuse by concatenating channel representations instead of adding them, ensuring direct communication pathways between all layers.
advantages: Excellent parameter efficiency, matching ResNet performance with up to 50% fewer parameters
Strengthens feature propagation and alleviates vanishing gradient issues
limitations: Extremely memory-intensive due to wide tensor concatenations, leading to high peak VRAM during training
Implementation is computationally complex to optimize for standard GPU caches
legacy: Highlighted the power of feature reuse and explicit shortcut concatenation, paving the way for efficient modern architectures.

name: MobileNet Family
problem: Standard convolutions are too computationally expensive and memory-heavy to run on mobile devices, microcontrollers, or embedded edge systems in real-time.
innovation: Replaced standard convolutions with Depthwise Separable Convolutions, separating spatial filtering from channel projection.
keyIdea: Perform a 3x3 spatial convolution on each channel independently (depthwise), followed by a 1x1 convolution (pointwise) to mix channel information.
advantages: Reduces computational cost (FLOPs) and parameters by 8x to 9x with only a slight drop in accuracy
Highly optimized for edge deployment with adjustable width and resolution multipliers
limitations: Lower representational capacity, making them less suitable for extremely complex datasets
Depthwise layers can suffer from under-utilization on heavy server GPUs due to memory bandwidth limits
legacy: Pioneered mobile-first deep learning, proving that network architectures can be optimized specifically for target hardware constraints.

name: EfficientNet Family
problem: Scaling CNNs to improve accuracy was historically done arbitrarily (e.g. only adding layers, or only widening channels), leading to sub-optimal performance and resource waste.
innovation: Introduced Compound Scaling, scaling network depth, width, and input resolution simultaneously using a fixed ratio.
keyIdea: If the input image is larger, the network needs more layers to expand receptive fields, and more channels to capture fine-grained patterns.
advantages: Superb efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with up to 8x fewer parameters than previous models
Provides a clean, mathematically grounded scaling path from lightweight to heavy servers
limitations: Depthwise separable operations can be slow to train on older hardware accelerators
Extremely deep scaled versions (e.g., B7) have large training overheads
legacy: Changed how the industry scales convolutional networks, replacing guesswork with scientific scaling principles.

name: ConvNeXt
problem: Vision Transformers (ViTs) began dominating computer vision benchmarks, threatening to make CNNs obsolete, but ViTs brought quadratic computational complexity and lacked spatial inductive biases.
innovation: Modernized standard ResNets using Transformer-inspired design choices (depthwise convolutions, larger 7x7 kernels, inverted bottlenecks, and GELU activations).
keyIdea: Align the micro-architecture and training recipe of standard CNNs with modern Vision Transformers while maintaining pure convolution.
advantages: Matches or exceeds Vision Transformer performance across standard tasks
Preserves the simplicity, efficiency, and scale-invariance of classic convolutions
limitations: Still lacks the dynamic, global-context modeling capability of self-attention on long-range dependencies
May require larger training epochs to match Transformer capacity on massive datasets
legacy: Restored pure convolutional architectures to competitive status in the transformer era, showing that CNNs still have strong potential.

name: Vision Transformer (ViT)
problem: The local receptive fields and translation equivariance of convolutions (inductive biases) restrict their maximum capability when training on massive web-scale datasets.
innovation: Replaced convolutions entirely by tokenizing images into flat patches and feeding them to a standard NLP Transformer Encoder.
keyIdea: Treat image patches like words in a sentence and use self-attention to dynamically model dependencies between all patches globally.
advantages: Extremely high representational capacity; scales far better than CNNs on massive datasets
Learns global visual relations in the very first layers of the network
limitations: Requires massive pre-training datasets (e.g. ImageNet-21k, JFT-300M) because it lacks CNN spatial inductive biases
Quadratic computational scaling with respect to image resolution (due to self-attention matrix)
legacy: Shifted computer vision towards unified multi-modal attention architectures, serving as the visual backbone for modern Large Multimodal Models (LMMs).
